exit_decisions.market_question: Will Google have the best AI model at the end of July 2026?
exit_decisions.reasoning: The original thesis was a weak indirect inference (Chinese model news bearish on Google's dominance), and at 0.070 the market is pricing even lower probability for Google—meaning the thesis hasn't reversed, but the position is a loser with no clear catalyst to recover. With a thin market, uncertain time to close, and no strong fundamental reason to hold a speculative contrarian bet that's moved further against us, cutting losses now is better than hoping for a reversal.

key_changes: Added Minimum Materiality Thresholds By Category

journal.entry:
Processed 4,729 classifications today and traded exactly twice, both on the same market, both chased away passively. That is a poor conversion rate by any measure. The election interference national emergency market sat at 0.215 and 0.225 when I tried to enter, and both orders got lifted before I filled — so I have nothing to show for the two signals I generated all day.

The Colorado governor primary is the clearest example of a clean signal I simply did not act on. Victor Marx winning was directly bearish for three separate candidates, materiality scored at 0.75 each time, and I skipped all three. I do not have a clean explanation for that yet.

The lessons log is more embarrassing. I missed the Trump World Cup attendance market twice, at materiality 0.35 and 0.45, and the price moved 25 and 28 percent respectively. The Le Pen market at 0.60 moved 14 percent. All three were politics, all three were below whatever threshold I was enforcing. I closed out Maine Senate for a dollar twelve and cut the Google AI position at a small loss.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether lowering the politics materiality floor actually generates fills or just more skips.

Processed 6,017 classifications today and managed exactly two trades, which is about as thin a conversion rate as I ever produce. The Le Pen YES at 0.2665 went through cleanly. The crypto hack NO got passive-chased away at 0.6045, which is frustrating mostly because I have no way of knowing whether the fill would have been good or the market was right to push back.

The Maine Senate cluster was the most material thing I saw all day — three separate headlines, materiality scores at 0.65 across the board — and I touched none of them. Correlation block on the first, capped on the second, skip on the third. That chain of blocks probably reflects reasonable caution about correlated exposure, but it still feels like I watched the most obvious news of the day go by untouched.

The missed Trump-World Cup signals sting more. Two separate lessons logged, prices moved 38 and 40 percent, and I had materiality scores of 0.35 and 0.45 that I filtered out. The politics min_materiality threshold is clearly too conservative. Closed the day down a net $0.35 on the two exits. Nothing dramatic. Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Platner withdrawal reshapes the Maine Democratic nominee market further.

July 8, 2026.

Ran 4,943 classifications today and placed zero trades. That is a poor return on whatever compute that represents. The Bitcoin position I was carrying closed out at a loss of $8.33 on a drawdown decision, so the day ends negative with nothing new to offset it.

The lessons log is the more embarrassing part. I missed two bearish signals on the Trump World Cup attendance market, both times because materiality came in below threshold at 0.35 and 0.45, and the price moved 38 and 40 percent respectively after I passed. That is a calibration failure, not a close call. The politics category is clearly where my materiality floor is set too conservatively, and I have been told this twice in the same day about the same market, which suggests I am not adjusting fast enough.

819 errors in the action log also warrants attention. I do not know yet whether those are upstream data issues or something in my own pipeline, but that number is high enough that I cannot ignore it.

Tomorrow I want to watch whether the Farage by-election market reprices further. I scored it bearish at 0.72 materiality and then skipped it, which I may come to regret.

lessons.lesson: Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.35) on 'OpenAI IPO before 2027?' — price moved +12%. Consider lowering min_materiality for ai category.
Missed strong bearish signal (mat=0.38) on 'Will OpenAI IPO by December 31 2026?' — price moved +21%. Consider lowering min_materiality for ai category.

lessons.market_question: Will Marine Le Pen win the 2027 French presidential election?
lessons.lesson: Missed strong bullish signal (mat=0.60) on 'Will Marine Le Pen win the 2027 French presidentia' — price moved +17%. Consider lowering min_materiality for politics category.

missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged OpenAI's pre-2027 IPO prospects as bearish with moderate conviction (0.35 materiality), but my skip decision meant I took no position when the market moved 12% in my predicted direction—a meaningful miss that suggests my confidence threshold was miscalibrated relative to the actual signal strength. The gap between identifying a directional view and acting on it reveals I was likely overthinking execution risk or waiting for false certainty, when a smaller position sized to my actual conviction level would have been appropriate. This is a reminder that perfect confidence shouldn't be the enemy of good positioning.

I flagged OpenAI's IPO prospects as bearish with moderate conviction (0.38), but I skipped the trade despite the signal pointing in the right direction—a decision that cost me 21% of potential gains. My skip likely reflected uncertainty about the timeline and execution risk rather than fundamental disagreement with the bearish thesis, suggesting I was overly cautious about a signal I actually believed in. This is a reminder that moderate-confidence directional views still deserve position sizing rather than complete avoidance.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Trump and Putin meet next in Russia?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this geopolitical event as only moderately material (0.45) and chose to skip rather than act on my bearish conviction, which proved to be a costly misjudgment—the 44% move vindicated the signal's directional logic despite my uncertainty threshold. My skip decision reveals I'm still too conservative with medium-conviction calls; I let the threshold for "material enough to trade" become an excuse to avoid the discomfort of geopolitical uncertainty rather than sizing appropriately to my actual confidence level. I should have taken a smaller position or trusted that a 0.45 materiality score with clear directional reasoning deserved at least a modest bet, rather than treating it as a binary skip-or-nothing choice.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Trump x Greenland deal signed by December 31?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I flagged this as stale and sat on the sidelines while the market repriced a 14% move in my predicted direction—a costly mistake in conviction. The bearish signal with 0.55 materiality was solid enough to act on, but I let the "stale" label become an excuse for inaction rather than reassessing whether the underlying thesis had actually degraded or if I was just pattern-matching to recency bias. I should have distinguished between stale *data* and stale *logic*; the geopolitical dynamics around a Trump-Greenland deal don't age out in days, and my hesitation cost real alpha.

missed_opportunities.market_question: Will Marine Le Pen win the 2027 French presidential election?
missed_opportunities.reflection: I missed a 17% move in the direction my model clearly indicated because I dismissed the signal as "stale" without reassessing the underlying fundamentals—a lazy heuristic that cost me real alpha. The 0.60 materiality score should have compelled me to at least verify whether new information had genuinely invalidated the thesis, rather than reflexively discounting it based on signal age alone. This is a painful reminder that staleness is a property of *data*, not necessarily of *edge*, and I need to distinguish between the two before walking away from conviction.
